nativeRange.habitat: Seaward rocky reefs and coral reefs at depths of 15–100 m

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on zooplankton in the wild, making it a planktivore within the otherwise sponge- and algae-heavy Pomacanthidae family. Has been documented accepting enriched frozen mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, high-quality marine angelfish preparations, spirulina, and marine algae. Generally tends to adapt well to prepared foods compared with many other angelfish. Feeding 2–3 times daily is generally recommended.

summary: A striking, reef-safe angelfish from the Western Pacific that is generally considered one of the more aquarium-friendly angelfishes due to its planktivorous diet and peaceful temperament. Males and females have dramatically different coloration.
careNotes:
Genicanthus semifasciatus belongs to the only planktivorous genus within Pomacanthidae, which makes it generally considered safer around corals and invertebrates than most other angelfish. Males display a bright yellow-orange face with vertical black bars on a white body and a characteristic lyrate (swallowtail) caudal fin, while females are creamy-white with black markings and a somewhat less elongated tail — the sexual dimorphism is so pronounced that males and females are sometimes mistaken for different species in the trade.

In the wild, the species has been observed living in small groups or harems consisting of one dominant male and several females. Only one male per aquarium is generally recommended; males have been known to be aggressive toward other males of the same genus. Can be kept as a mated pair or as a group of females; introducing a juvenile male into a female group is one documented approach for forming a harem in captivity.

An active, open-water swimmer that tends to need ample horizontal swimming space. Sources generally recommend a minimum of 500 L to accommodate its movement and social structure, especially if maintaining a pair or small group. Best introduced into an established reef aquarium with good water quality and sufficient live rock for hiding places.

The key to successful keeping has been reported as acquiring smaller juveniles, which tend to acclimate more readily than full-sized adults. Lifespan has been reported at 10–15 years in well-maintained aquariums.

Water parameter notes: temperature was averaged across four sources (LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6°C, FishBase 21.9–27.7°C, TankFacts 24.4–26.7°C, SaltwaterFish.com 23.9–27.8°C), yielding a consensus range of approximately 23–27°C. pH averaged from three sources (8.0–8.4). Tank size averaged from two sources (125 gal / 473 L and 120 gal / 454 L), rounded up to 500 L to account for the species' active swimming and social needs.
breedingNotes: Genicanthus semifasciatus is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals are born female and the dominant fish in a group can transition to male. In the wild, groups form permanent harems of one male and several females. Captive spawning has been reported but is uncommon in the hobby. The species is believed to release pelagic eggs in open water; raising larvae is generally considered very difficult in home aquarium conditions.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (21.0 cm max), temperature range (21.9–27.7°C), native range (southern Japan to Taiwan and northern Philippines), taxonomy, and harem/protogynous social structure. Temperature range broader than LiveAquaria; averaged with other sources.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6–8 in / 15–20 cm), temperature (75–82°F / 23.9–27.8°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), tank size (120 gal / 454 L), lifespan (10–15 years). Used in averaging high-stakes parameter fields.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (76–80°F / 24.4–26.7°C), pH (7.8–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.026), kH (8–12 dKH). Used in averaging temperature and salinity; pH min (7.8) pulled the averaged minimum lower to 8.0.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems in the wild. Captive spawning has been reported but larval rearing is generally considered very difficult. Acquiring a juvenile group and allowing a harem to form naturally is the most commonly documented captive approach.
